_comment: Production database choice with full rationale, alternatives, trade-offs, a 90-day check-in, and decision relations (triggered_by + caused). Use as a template for any significant infra choice you want to be able to revisit with full context.
title: Postgres for production, SQLite for local-dev only
description: Production runs managed Postgres (single primary + hot replica). Local development and CI use SQLite via a thin compatibility layer for the subset of features both engines share. No SQLite in production, no Postgres requirement for a contributor's first `make dev`.
rationale: Two pressures pointed the same way. Production needs concurrent writers, row-level locking, and the JSON/full-text/geo features Postgres ships with. Local-dev needs zero-install onboarding so a new contributor can clone the repo and run tests in under a minute. SQLite is fine for the test-fixture data we use locally; Postgres is the right call for live traffic.
trade_offs: Two SQL dialects to keep in sync — any query using Postgres-only features (JSONB ops, CTEs in certain shapes, LATERAL joins) needs a SQLite-compatible alternative or a guard
Bugs that only reproduce under Postgres-specific isolation behaviour will not show up in local tests
Schema migrations must be tested against both engines in CI
alternatives_considered: SQLite for production — rejected: write concurrency ceiling too low for our target load
Postgres for local-dev too — rejected: onboarding friction; new contributors abandon at the install step
MySQL — rejected: weaker JSON support and we already have Postgres expertise in-house
DynamoDB — rejected: vendor lock-in, weak local-dev story, lacks SQL
next_step: Wire up a CI matrix job that runs the full test suite against both SQLite and Postgres on every PR
restore_step: If Postgres goes down: failover to hot replica (automatic, RTO ~60s). If both are lost: restore latest WAL-backed snapshot from object storage; document last-known-good RPO is 5 minutes.

evidence_links.notes: Initial schema migrates cleanly on both engines; smoke tests green

triggered_by.title: Adopt SQL as the primary persistence layer (rejected NoSQL/document stores)
triggered_by.notes: Choosing SQL in the parent decision forced the question of which engine

caused.title: Adopt sqlx + compile-time query checking
caused.notes: Picking Postgres + SQLite for dev made compile-time query verification a hard requirement
